subject: English
title: Extended Reading Passages with Questions – Magnifying Glass
instructions: Read each passage carefully. Answer the questions using information only from the given paragraph.

passages.paragraph: A magnifying glass is a simple tool that helps us see tiny objects more clearly. When we look through it, small things appear larger and reveal details that are normally hidden from our eyes.
questions: What is a magnifying glass?
What does a magnifying glass help us see?
How do objects appear through a magnifying glass?
Why are tiny objects easier to see with it?
What does the magnifying glass reveal?

passages.paragraph: The poet uses a magnifying glass to explore the natural world. By looking closely at ordinary things, the poet discovers that nature is full of surprising patterns and details.
questions: What tool does the poet use?
What does the poet explore with the magnifying glass?
What does the poet discover about nature?
Why does the poet look closely at ordinary things?
What does this observation reveal?

passages.paragraph: When the poet looks at a scrap of chalk through the magnifying glass, it appears full of tiny shells. This surprising sight shows that even simple objects contain hidden structures.
questions: What object does the poet observe?
How does the chalk appear through the magnifying glass?
What does the poet notice inside the chalk?
What does this observation show about simple objects?
Why is the sight surprising?

passages.paragraph: An inch of moss appears like a small forest when seen through the magnifying glass. Tiny stems and shapes look like trees and plants, creating the image of a miniature world.
questions: What does the poet observe in this passage?
How does moss appear through the magnifying glass?
What do the tiny stems look like?
What image does the moss create?
Why does the poet call it a miniature world?

passages.paragraph: A drop of water also reveals surprising activity when magnified. The poet compares it to a hive of bees because it seems full of movement and energy.
questions: What object is described in the passage?
What happens when the drop of water is magnified?
What does the poet compare the drop of water to?
Why is this comparison used?
What does the drop of water appear to contain?

passages.paragraph: The poet carefully watches a spider spinning its web. Through the magnifying glass, the spider’s claws and movements appear detailed and powerful.
questions: Which creature does the poet observe?
What is the spider doing?
How do the spider’s claws appear?
Why are the spider’s movements interesting?
What tool helps the poet see these details?

passages.paragraph: The spider creates its web using fine silk from its spinnerets. This delicate thread forms a strong web that traps small insects that fly into it.
questions: What does the spider use to create its web?
Where does the silk come from?
What kind of web does the spider create?
What happens to insects that enter the web?
Why is the web important for the spider?

passages.paragraph: Through careful observation, the poet realizes that even the smallest objects in nature have complex structures. A magnifying glass allows people to appreciate these hidden details.
questions: What does the poet realize?
What kind of structures do small objects have?
What tool helps reveal these structures?
Why is observation important?
What does the magnifying glass help people appreciate?

passages.paragraph: The poet believes that curiosity helps us discover the wonders of nature. When we observe the world closely, we can find beauty even in the smallest things.
questions: What quality helps people discover nature’s wonders?
What happens when we observe the world closely?
Where can beauty be found?
Why is curiosity important?
What lesson does the poet share?

passages.paragraph: The poem teaches that careful observation reveals hidden beauty in nature. With curiosity and attention, even tiny objects can become fascinating and meaningful.
questions: What does the poem teach?
What reveals hidden beauty in nature?
What qualities help people make discoveries?
How can tiny objects appear when observed carefully?
Why is this message important?
